name: Paria River
short_description: BLM public land along the Paria River where you can collect agate, jasper, and petrified wood with no permit required. Beautiful high-elevation Utah canyon with moderate rockhounding difficulty.
description:
The Paria River in Kane County flows through striking red rock canyons at 5,695 feet elevation, offering excellent rockhounding opportunities on BLM land. This remote area is known for chalcedony nodules, colorful jasper pieces, and petrified wood fragments scattered across benches and wash bottoms. The terrain features moderate slopes with exposed geological formations dating to the Cretaceous and Tertiary periods, when ancient marine and continental deposits laid down the layers you see today. Collectors should expect a scenic but rugged experience in a lesser-known Utah rockhounding location that rewards patient searching along canyon washes and hillside exposures.

The geology here reflects the Colorado Plateau's complex stratigraphy. Silica-rich fluids from ancient hydrothermal activity filled fractures and cavities, creating the agate and chalcedony nodules you find eroding out of hillsides. The petrified wood comes from Cretaceous river systems that flowed through this region millions of years ago, burying plant material that was slowly replaced by silica. The red coloring in many pieces indicates iron oxide content from the surrounding Navajo and Entrada sandstone formations.

Water access is variable along the Paria River, so bring plenty to drink. The area can be hot during summer months and cold in winter, making spring and fall the ideal collecting seasons. Early morning trips allow you to search before afternoon heat sets in and give you time to explore multiple locations along the river corridor.
history: This region sits within the Colorado Plateau, where Cretaceous-age river systems deposited sand and silt that buried wood and organic material. Silica-rich groundwater later replaced these materials, creating the petrified wood and agate nodules found here today. The distinctive red coloring of many specimens comes from iron oxide staining during millions of years of weathering and erosion.
tips: Search along the Paria River wash bottom and the benches immediately above the water line, where gravity has concentrated heavier agate and chalcedony pieces. Look for translucent to opaque nodules the size of walnuts to grapefruit, often with a white or tan chalky exterior coating that signals quality material inside. Bring a small hammer and chisel to test pieces you find - true agate will ring cleanly when struck, while softer material will crumble. The best collecting is in early morning before heat becomes intense; start at accessible points where the road meets the canyon and work gradually upstream.
terrain_notes: Elevation 5,695 feet with moderate slopes along canyon walls and benches. Terrain is rocky with loose talus, red sandstone outcrops, and scattered vegetation including juniper and desert shrub. Walking distance from parking to collecting areas typically ranges from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on road access. Footing is uneven and rocky; sturdy boots with ankle support are strongly recommended.
best_season: October - April. Summer temperatures can exceed 95 degrees Fahrenheit in the exposed canyon, while winter can bring cold and occasional snow at this elevation. Spring and fall offer ideal 60-75 degree temperatures and lower intense sun exposure for all-day rockhounding.
written_directions: From Georgetown, take Utah State Route 12 southeast for approximately 18 miles toward Paria. Continue past Paria toward the Paria River drainage. Look for BLM access points and informal pullouts along the river corridor. The last 5-10 miles may be rough dirt road with washboard conditions - high-clearance vehicle recommended. Park at any established turnout near the canyon rim and walk down to the river benches. GPS coordinates 37.529756, -112.04377 mark a typical access point.
road_conditions: Final approach is rough dirt road with washboard surface and potential high-clearance requirement. Earlier sections via State Route 12 are paved.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and turnouts along the river road. No developed parking lot. Space is limited at most locations; plan to arrive early, especially in spring and fall.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection allowed. No motorized excavation or powered tools. Remove only loose material and specimens you can hand-carry. Do not disturb natural formations or dig into solid rock faces. Leave gates as you find them. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or powered equipment
No digging into solid rock
Remove only loose material
Pack out all trash
Leave no surface disturbance
nearest_services: Georgetown has minimal services. Kanab, Utah (approximately 35 miles west) has gas, food, lodging, and supplies. Paria is a small ghost town area with no services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate difficulty terrain with uneven, rocky footing and loose talus. No wheelchair access. Moderate slope requires sustained walking uphill and downhill. Sturdy hiking boots with ankle support required. Not suitable for those with limited mobility. Children should be supervised around cliff edges and loose rock.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can I find rockhounding at Paria River near Georgetown, Utah?
faq.answer: You can find agate, chalcedony nodules, colorful jasper, and petrified wood at Paria River. The agate and chalcedony typically occur as nodules ranging from walnut to grapefruit size, often with white or tan exterior coatings. Petrified wood pieces are scattered throughout the washes and benches, particularly in areas with exposed Cretaceous-age sediments.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Paria River BLM land in Kane County?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Paria River. The area is public BLM land managed under FLPMA, which allows personal use collection without a permit. However, you must follow the 25-pound daily limit and collect only loose material - no motorized excavation or commercial use is allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Paria River, Utah?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for Paria River. Summer temperatures often exceed 95 degrees in the exposed canyon, making collecting uncomfortable and potentially dangerous. Spring and fall offer 60-75 degree temperatures, lower sun intensity, and more comfortable all-day rockhounding conditions. Winter is possible but can bring snow and cold at 5,695 feet elevation.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Paria River and is it suitable for families with kids?
faq.answer: Paria River is moderate difficulty rockhounding suitable for families with children ages 8 and up. The terrain features rocky, uneven footing and loose talus along moderate slopes, but most collecting areas are accessible within 0.5 to 2 miles of parking. Children should wear sturdy boots, be supervised around cliff edges, and the hike is not suitable for very young children or those with limited mobility.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring for rockhounding along the Paria River?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer and chisel to test pieces - true agate rings cleanly when struck. Pack sturdy hiking boots with ankle support, plenty of water (at least 2-3 liters), sun protection, and a collection bag. The rough dirt road approach requires a high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle. Wear long sleeves and pants to protect against sharp rocks and desert vegetation.

faq.question: How do I find quality agate and petrified wood at Paria River rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Search the Paria River wash bottoms and benches immediately above the water line, where heavier materials concentrate. Look for nodules with white or tan exterior coatings - these indicate good quality agate inside. Test suspicious pieces with a hammer; agate will ring cleanly. Petrified wood pieces often show wood grain texture and are scattered widely. Early morning collecting before heat intensifies gives you the best light and energy for a full day of searching.

meta_title: Paria River Rockhounding - Find Agate & Jasper in Utah
meta_description: Collect agate, jasper, and petrified wood at Paria River on BLM public land near Georgetown, Utah. No permit needed, 25 lb daily limit. Perfect for families.
